content.text: Why RAG Systems Need Master Data Management

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems are only as accurate as their grounding sources. When those sources are drawn from multiple enterprise systems—CRM, ERP, HRMS, wikis, ticketing systems—each system maintains its own representation of shared real-world entities: products, customers, employees, locations, policies. Without a Master Data Management (MDM) layer, the same entity appears under different names, IDs, and attribute values across systems. The RAG index inherits this inconsistency, and the AI agent inherits it from the index.

The result is concrete grounding failures: an agent quotes a product price from a stale ERP record when the CRM has the updated price; an agent refers to a customer by two different names in the same session because the CRM and ticketing system disagree; an agent retrieves two conflicting policy documents and cannot determine which is authoritative. MDM eliminates these failures by establishing a single canonical representation—the golden record—before content reaches the grounding pipeline.
MDM Hub Pattern for Grounding Sources
The diagram below shows the MDM hub pattern applied to a RAG grounding pipeline, with source systems on the left and the grounding pipeline on the right.

attrs.alt: MDM hub pattern for RAG grounding sources showing four source systems feeding the MDM hub, producing a golden record, then feeding the grounding pipeline

content.text: Source systems feed the MDM hub, which performs match-and-merge deduplication and assigns a canonical mdm_id. The golden record produced by the hub is the sole input to the grounding pipeline. The bottom row shows two grounding failure patterns without MDM—duplicate records with conflicting prices, and stale index state after a price update—both resolved when MDM is in place.
Core MDM Concepts Applied to RAG

Entity resolution: The process of identifying that records in different source systems refer to the same real-world entity. For RAG grounding, this means matching a product record in Salesforce with its counterpart in SAP using deterministic rules (exact SKU match) or probabilistic algorithms (fuzzy name matching, address normalization).

Golden record: The single authoritative record for each entity, produced by applying survivorship rules to the matched source records. A survivorship rule specifies which source system wins for each attribute: price from ERP, customer contact from CRM, headcount from HRMS.

Canonical ID (mdm_id): A stable identifier assigned to each golden record. All RAG chunks derived from that entity carry the mdm_id in their vector store metadata. When the golden record is updated, the mdm_id is preserved; only the content changes. This enables targeted re-indexing without full corpus rebuilds.

Lineage: The MDM hub records which source records contributed to each golden record, and which attributes came from which source. This lineage is stored in the vector metadata alongside the mdm_id, enabling the AI agent to cite the authoritative source for any fact it retrieves.
Entity Domain Mapping for AI Grounding
MDM typically governs several entity domains. The table below maps each domain to its primary grounding use case in enterprise AI and its most common conflict risk.
MDM Domain
Grounding Use Case
Key Conflict Risk
Product
Product specs, pricing, availability for AI sales/support assistants
Price discrepancies between CRM and ERP
Customer
Account history, contact info for AI relationship management
Duplicate customer records with different contact details
Employee
Org chart, role expertise for AI internal directory and routing
Org changes in HRMS not reflected in Active Directory
Location
Office locations, jurisdiction mapping for AI compliance queries
Address formatting differences across systems
Policy/Process
Authoritative procedures for AI compliance and HR assistants
Multiple versions of the same policy in different wikis
Survivorship Rules for Grounding

Survivorship rules determine which source system's value for a given attribute is authoritative when conflicts arise. These rules are the most governance-sensitive part of MDM for AI: an incorrect survivorship rule will cause the AI agent to consistently retrieve wrong information. Rules must be explicitly owned by a named domain steward and reviewed whenever the source-system landscape changes.

The fallback when no priority list exists uses the order of source_values keys, which is insertion-order in Python dicts. In production, explicitly enumerate every domain-attribute pair that matters to AI grounding rather than relying on fallback behavior. Undocumented fallbacks are a silent correctness risk.
Change Events and RAG Index Synchronization

The MDM hub must publish change events whenever a golden record is updated. These events drive the grounding pipeline's re-indexing logic. Without change-event-driven synchronization, the RAG index accumulates stale data over time as source systems are updated. The staleness accumulates silently: there are no retrieval errors, only subtly incorrect answers.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Literal

@dataclass
class MDMChangeEvent:
    mdm_id: str
    entity_domain: str                  # product, customer, employee, policy
    change_type: Literal["create", "update", "merge", "delete"]
    changed_attributes: list[str]       # which attributes changed
    golden_record_version: int          # monotonically increasing version number
    occurred_at: datetime
    source_system: str                  # which source triggered the change

def handle_mdm_change(event: MDMChangeEvent, rag_pipeline, mdm_hub) -> None:
    """Re-index all chunks associated with the changed golden record."""
    if event.change_type == "delete":
        rag_pipeline.purge_entity(mdm_id=event.mdm_id)
        return

    if event.change_type == "merge":
        # The retired ID's chunks must be deleted. The surviving ID gets
        # its own update event, so handle only the retired side here.
        rag_pipeline.purge_entity(mdm_id=event.mdm_id)
        return

    # create and update: re-ingest the current golden record
    golden_record = mdm_hub.get_golden_record(event.mdm_id)
    rag_pipeline.ingest(
        content=golden_record.to_text(),
        metadata={
            "mdm_id": event.mdm_id,
            "domain": event.entity_domain,
            "version": event.golden_record_version,
            "source_ids": golden_record.source_ids,
            "indexed_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        }
    )

The merge case requires careful handling. When two golden records are merged, the hub retires one mdm_id and keeps the other. The change event for the retired ID must trigger a purge; the surviving ID will receive a separate update event to refresh its chunks with the merged attributes. A single change event does not cover both sides — subscribing only to one misses the purge.
MDM Metadata in the Vector Store
Every chunk ingested into the vector store must carry MDM-derived metadata. This metadata enables filtered retrieval, staleness detection, and source attribution at query time.

mdm_id: The canonical entity identifier. All chunks from the same golden record share this ID. Enables deleting and rewriting all chunks for an entity when the golden record changes, without touching unrelated chunks.

golden_record_version: The version of the golden record at index time. At retrieval time, the agent middleware can compare this against the current golden record version and discard or flag chunks that are more than N versions behind.

source_ids: A map from source system name to the source record ID that contributed to this chunk. Enables the agent to cite the authoritative source: "This price is sourced from ERP record SKU-001234."
entity_domain: The MDM domain (product, customer, employee, policy). Used by the retrieval layer to scope queries to relevant domains based on query intent classification.

indexed_at: ISO 8601 timestamp of when the chunk was last written into the vector store. Used in staleness dashboards to identify chunks that have not been re-indexed recently relative to how frequently the source system changes.
Staleness Detection at Query Time

Even with change-event-driven re-indexing, chunks can become stale if the change event pipeline has lag or gaps. A staleness check at query time prevents the agent from relying on chunks whose version is known to be behind the current golden record.

The max_version_lag threshold is a governance decision. A value of 0 means only the very latest indexed version is used; a value of 2 tolerates minor replication delay for non-critical domains. High-risk domains (pricing, contract terms) should use a lag of 0 or 1; descriptive reference data can tolerate larger lags.
Match Confidence and Unresolved Conflicts

Entity resolution is not always deterministic. When two records have similar but not identical identifiers, the MDM hub assigns a match confidence score. Records below the confidence threshold are flagged as potential duplicates and routed to a human steward for manual review. Until resolved, the MDM hub should not merge them into a single golden record.

For RAG purposes, unresolved duplicates mean two separate golden records with separate mdm_ids are indexed independently. When the agent retrieves both in the same query, the presence of duplicate records with conflicting attribute values is itself information: the agent should surface the conflict to the user rather than silently picking one. The AI middleware layer can detect duplicate-suspect mdm_ids and inject a conflict warning into the prompt context.

Match confidence thresholds require calibration per domain. A 90% confidence threshold for product matching may be appropriate but the same threshold for customer matching may be too permissive in contexts where a mistaken merge could expose one customer's data to another. Domain stewards must review confidence distribution histograms when setting thresholds, not guess.
Scoping MDM Investment to High-Risk Domains
Full MDM is a major enterprise program. For RAG governance, prioritize MDM investment by the risk of incorrect grounding in each domain.

Priority 1 — transactional data with external impact: Pricing, availability, contract terms. Errors here cause financial or legal damage. Full MDM with real-time change events and staleness detection required. max_version_lag = 0 or 1.

Priority 2 — regulatory and compliance documents: Policies, procedures, regulatory guidance. Stale or conflicting versions can lead to incorrect compliance advice. MDM with version-controlled golden records, expiry dates, and mandatory re-indexing on publication.

Priority 3 — reference and descriptive data: Product descriptions, team bios, location information. Lower risk of financial impact. Batch MDM with daily synchronization is sufficient; max_version_lag = 5 or higher.
Operational MDM Patterns for Enterprise AI
Three operational patterns emerge when MDM is deployed specifically to govern AI grounding sources, each with distinct infrastructure requirements.

Consolidation hub: All source systems write to a central MDM hub. The hub produces golden records. The RAG grounding pipeline reads only from the hub. This is the cleanest architecture but requires upstream system integration work and a dedicated MDM platform (Informatica, Stibo, Reltio, or custom).

Registry/federated MDM: The MDM system maintains a cross-reference index of IDs across source systems without physically consolidating records. The grounding pipeline queries the registry for the canonical ID, then fetches the winning attribute values from the source systems using the survivorship rules at query time. More complex retrieval logic but avoids a full MDM hub build.

Lightweight metadata overlay: For teams without MDM infrastructure, a metadata layer adds mdm_id and survivorship annotations to existing source records at ingestion time. A reconciliation job runs nightly to flag conflicts without resolving them. The AI agent surfaces flagged conflicts to users rather than silently choosing. This is not real MDM but reduces the worst grounding errors at lower cost.
Golden Record Data Model for AI Grounding

A golden record for AI grounding purposes carries more metadata than a typical MDM golden record, because the grounding pipeline needs to produce text representations and the retrieval layer needs to perform post-retrieval filtering. The following data model covers the minimum fields required.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class GoldenRecord:
    mdm_id: str                             # Canonical stable identifier
    entity_domain: str                      # product, customer, employee, policy
    version: int                            # Monotonically increasing
    created_at: datetime
    updated_at: datetime
    attributes: dict[str, Any]              # Attribute name -> winning value
    attribute_sources: dict[str, str]       # Attribute name -> winning source system
    source_ids: dict[str, str]              # Source system -> source record ID
    match_confidence: float                 # 0.0–1.0; 1.0 for deterministic matches
    steward_notes: str = ""                 # Human steward comments
    is_active: bool = True                  # False after delete or permanent merge
    expiry_date: datetime | None = None     # For policy records with known expiry

    def to_text(self) -> str:
        """Produce a human-readable text representation for grounding."""
        lines = [
            f"Entity: {self.entity_domain} (mdm_id={self.mdm_id}, v{self.version})",
            f"Last updated: {self.updated_at.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}",
        ]
        for attr, value in self.attributes.items():
            source = self.attribute_sources.get(attr, "unknown")
            lines.append(f"{attr}: {value}  [source: {source}]")
        return "\n".join(lines)

The to_text() method bridges the MDM layer and the RAG ingestion pipeline. It produces a plaintext representation that includes source attribution inline, so that when the chunk is retrieved and placed in the agent's context, the attribution is already present. The agent does not need to look up source_ids separately to cite its sources.
MDM Lineage for AI Audit and Compliance

When an enterprise AI agent provides a factual response grounded in retrieved content, regulators and internal auditors may ask: where did that fact come from? MDM lineage provides the answer traceable back to the original source system record.

Lineage chain: Source system record → MDM match-and-merge → golden record (with version) → grounding pipeline chunk → vector store embedding → retrieval result → agent context → agent response. Each link in this chain should be traceable through IDs and timestamps stored in the agent's session log.

Audit queries the MDM hub can answer: Which source records were merged to produce this golden record? When was this attribute's value last changed and by which source? Was this golden record version the current version at the time the agent generated its response? The hub must store a version history, not just the current state.

Point-in-time reconstruction: For compliance investigations, the MDM hub must be able to reconstruct the golden record as it existed at any past timestamp. This requires storing the full version history with timestamps rather than only the current version. Without this, it is impossible to audit whether the agent used an accurate golden record at the time of the disputed response.

Retention policy alignment: MDM version history retention must align with the retention policies of the regulated data categories present in the golden records. For GDPR, right-to-erasure requests must propagate from source system deletion through MDM (retire the golden record) and into the vector store (purge all chunks with that mdm_id).
Key Takeaways

RAG grounding conflicts—duplicate entities, stale values, conflicting attributes—arise when source systems are indexed directly without MDM. MDM resolves these by producing a single golden record per entity before grounding.
The MDM hub assigns a canonical mdm_id to each golden record; all RAG chunks carry this ID in their vector store metadata to enable targeted re-indexing without full corpus rebuilds.
Survivorship rules must be explicitly defined and owned by domain stewards; they determine which source system's attribute value is authoritative when sources disagree.
MDM change events must drive immediate re-indexing for high-risk domains; merge events require handling both the retired ID (purge) and the surviving ID (re-index) as separate operations.
Staleness detection at query time provides a safety net when the change event pipeline has lag; max_version_lag thresholds should be set per domain based on risk level.

Not all domains require a full consolidation hub. A registry/federated model or lightweight metadata overlay can deliver most of the grounding quality benefits at a fraction of the infrastructure cost for teams starting out.
